title: Remember when you wanted to translate COBOL into modern languages? That thread is still sitting there.
html:
Question

"Find a substantial thread in 2022-2023 you probably haven't thought about in months — bring it back."

The thread: legacy-code translation as an AI wedge

In May 2023, sitting in a list called IDEAS: between "AR for concerts" and "rent marry me signs," you wrote this single line:

"Translating cobol to modern language for financial infrastructure"

(Source: raw/apple-notes/2023-05-13-ideas.md, modified through Nov 29 2023.)

Five months earlier, in 2022 10 25 ahmad jamal stream, you'd already articulated the underlying philosophy that this idea was downstream of: "Take existing things if equivalent value, but make them with 1/10 less resources and significantly cheaper." You were also, in the same note, brainstorming ChatGPT wedges weeks after the model launched. You had two of the three ingredients — a thesis (cheap-replication of expensive things), and a tool (LLMs). The third ingredient was a target. You named one: COBOL.

You then never came back to it. I checked. The string cobol appears in exactly two notes in the entire vault — the 2022-10-25 polymath dump and the 2023-05-13 IDEAS list. Zero hits in any 2024, 2025, or 2026 note. Not in 2024 05 13 things (the "decide on" startup-thesis menu). Not in 2024 12 21 todos (the BroMenu spec era). Not in 2026 01 07 rankett meetup prep guide. Not even as a passing reference. The thread went dark in late 2023 and never came back up.

Where it left off (mid-2023):

  • You had named the target: financial infrastructure stuck on COBOL.
  • You had the philosophical frame: take existing things, do them with 1/10 the resources.
  • You had begun playing with GPT as a wedge.
  • You were about to leave for jordan/israel/egypt (the Jordan trip interrupted the founder-thinking thread for ~3 months).
  • You came back to the OSU grind, and within months were absorbed in finals + India/Nepal + Shanghai visa logistics. The idea got buried under the China chapter.
  • By the time you re-emerged founder-Yahir in late 2024, the idea you reached for was a fitness app (BroMenu), not a legacy-code translator. Then Rankett in 2026.

What's changed since you put it down:

  • COBOL modernization is now a publicly-named category — IBM/Watsonx Code Assistant for Z launched 2023; multiple YC batches in 2024-2025 included COBOL-to-Java/Python translators; the U.S. federal government has open RFPs for it. Your 2023 thesis went from "obscure idea" to "named market" in 18 months. (Grind in an obscure field, then when opportunity comes you will be ahead — your own line — ironic that the wave came and you weren't there.)
  • Your ghost white label / whitelabel strategy / agency-as-distribution-channel architecture from rankett is exactly the GTM shape that would work for legacy-code translation: banks and insurance carriers don't buy SaaS, they buy through systems integrators. The Rankett architecture is reusable.
  • You now ship LLM-orchestrated workflows for paying customers. In 2023 this was theoretical. In 2026 it's your day job.

What this is and isn't.
This isn't me saying go pivot. Rankett is the bet, and the self funded no runway reality means thread-switching is the most expensive thing you could do. This is me saying: there's a fully-formed founder-thread you wrote into existence in 2023, in your own voice, that the 2026 version of you has the exact tools to execute, and you have not thought about it in 30+ months. It's sitting in raw/apple-notes/2023-05-13-ideas.md in a list with 18 other ideas. Most of them were noise. This one wasn't.

The question isn't "should you do it." The question is: did you forget it because it wasn't actually a fit, or did you forget it because your attention got absorbed by visa paperwork and the China move and you never came back to evaluate? The answer matters. If it's the former, you can finally cross it off the list. If it's the latter, it goes in the bucket and gets a 30-minute weekly question against it: who already won this, and is that win durable?

You might want to think about this because…

The 2022-08-29 line — "Grind in an obscure field, then when opportunity comes you will be ahead" — is a load-bearing piece of your own founder-philosophy, and the COBOL idea is the literal example you would have given of it in 2023. The fact that the wave came (multiple YC batches, federal RFPs, IBM product launches) and you weren't grinding on the field you'd already named is not a failure — it's data. It tells you something about which threads your attention actually keeps and which ones it loses, regardless of how good they are. That's diagnostic information about your own attention you can use forward, not backward. Your past self had a real instinct here. He just got busy.

Sources

  • raw/apple-notes/2023-05-13-ideas.md — the original IDEAS list (the COBOL line + 17 sibling ideas including "AR for concerts," "AI girlfriends," "USE GPT BUILDER TO CREATE A GPT WRAPPER APPLICATION")
  • 2022 10 25 ahmad jamal stream — "Take existing things if equivalent value, but make them with 1/10 less resources and significantly cheaper" + ChatGPT wedge brainstorming weeks after launch
  • raw/apple-notes/2022-08-29-grind-in-an-obscure-field-then-when-opportunity-comes-you-wi.md — "Grind in an obscure field, then when opportunity comes you will be ahead. Happened with niche law practice, software programming, crypto."
  • raw/apple-notes/2023-07-05-startup-advice.md — Antonio Garcia Martinez "monomaniacally focus on one thing" line (which you cited again in 2026-01-11, but never applied to the COBOL bet)
  • 2024 05 13 things — the "decide on" thesis menu of mid-2024 (asset tokenization, security/human-interface disruption, truck driver ads, smart-home integration) — notice COBOL is NOT on this list, and neither is anything else from the 2023 IDEAS file
  • 2026 01 07 rankett meetup prep guide — the ghost white label / agency-channel architecture that would be reusable for a legacy-code play
  • Concepts: nonobvious · asking the right question · disruption · whitelabel strategy
